speaker: interviewer
text: Today's March 28, 1997. I'm here with George Riechfeld. The interviewer's name is Jill Martin. We're in Boston, Massachusetts in the United States of America. And the language of the interview is English. Today is March 28th, 1997. We're here with George Riechfeld. My name is Jill Martin. We're in Boston, Massachusetts in the United States of America. And the language of the interview is English. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is George Riechfeld. Before, was Riechenfeld. And now, it's George Riechfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Riechenfeld when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, it was Riechenfeld, but now it's Riechfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Chicago, Illinois, 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Eddie, Eddie Riechenfeld. That time, it was Revesz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Revesz, R-E-V-E-S-Z. When my father didn't want to have a German, he took up a Hungarian name, Revesz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my God. My father was 55 years old when he died in 1921. In the 60s, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Weber. Sophie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sophie Weber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was born 10 years before my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Younger, 10 years-- yeah, after, excuse me. She was 10 years younger than my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born 1876?

speaker: interviewee
text: '76, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in [NON-ENGLISH] Yugoslavia and the Hungarian border. My mother was born in-- one second, [HUNGARIAN], in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did they come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- my-- my parents were married-- you want to know the whole-- my parents were married 16 years with no children. And my parents must come to United States in the 19-- my mother was 17 years old when she got married. And in 19-- 16 years later, she come to United States to Chicago. What I was born in 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents come 1906 to United States, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked by Oscar Meyer in Chicago on Cedric Street, a meat cutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work. Yes, and that was housework. We had about six, seven people working by-- living by us. And my mother used to cook for them. And they lived there. We had a 17-- we had seven or eight bedrooms. That time, we lived 1412 Cedric Street in Chicago on the North Side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to school in North Hudson Street in Chicago on the North Side. And my school teacher's name was Ms. Daly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What grades did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the sixth grade or the seventh grade. When my father died in 1921, we went back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your life in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life in Chicago was pretty good. I had all Hungarian friends, most of them. And my father once thought-- they were very young, these little Hungarian gypsies playing violin. And my father got so crazy about it, he went right away and bought me a violin that I should learn to play violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned it a while for a couple years how long my father didn't die. And then when my father died, 1921, would come back. My father died June-- January the 21st. And we come back September 21st from Chicago to Hungary. And my mother put my violin in the thing. And when we got back to Hungary, it broke all in pieces. After that, I never played.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents speak Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always Hungarian-- not Jewish, not German, just Hungarian. I was in the school. I was playing the flute-- is that the flute?

speaker: interviewer
text: The flute, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The flute, I was playing it, a little baseball.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel connected to Hungarian culture growing up in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: How should I explain you that? My parents and all their friends were Hungarian. They always spoke Hungarian. But in school, I was American. My name was [NON-ENGLISH], not George. But here, I got George. But my real name is [HUNGARIAN]. Is They can't spell that in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the kids in the school you went to school with? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very good friends. And I know I used to get a lot of cards for Valentine, and this and that. In school, I was very popular. I would-- I-- I-- I liked people. I was always joking and everything. And when we went away-- when I was a little lively, I used to put it in the girls in their hair, those things what sticks in it. I don't know what the hell they call this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berets? Or, I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what it was. They put it in. I was-- I was a very good student. I know that. They send me up eight, nine grade to talk about history and everything. I was a good-- a very good student.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolute nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not-- you didn't feel like you had a strong Jewish identity as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's your earliest memory of your life? What's the first thing you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we come-- when-- when-- I'll tell you what was my first thing that I never, never forget. Now, this is-- don't think I'm-- I'm-- I'm wise. I got my first pants, a long pants. And I said to my mother, Mommy, how am I going to make a sissy with the long pants? And we were making sissy with a girlfriend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH] who lived downstairs. That's the first-- now, this is the God's truth. [LAUGHS] Don't laugh of it. This is the truth. Then later on, we were coming home from school. We went together to school in North Hudson Street. We were coming home. This, I never forget. There was a guy with a little car and a goats in it. And he said, let me take a picture of you two guys. So we sit in. They took it, a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you live? And we told, we live on 1412 Cedric Street. And my mother was very surprised that I took a picture there with that-- sitting in that little-- and a goat in front of me. Tell me about your father. What was your father like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very strict man, very strict man. He said, you've got to be home. You've got to be home when the bell ring. And if I said, I don't like what my mother cooked, mister, you get away from the table. Now, this is a funny story. This is not a story. This is a true thing. We had a piggyback, me and [NON-ENGLISH], together. And we had $0.05, a nickle, a dime. And I always want-- you don't remember. You're too young for that, wore this Indian feather hat, with the Indian's hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked my father, can I have one? He says, if you bring me a good card from school, I'm going to buy you one. So what's a kid to tell a lie? One day, [NON-ENGLISH] mother come up, and says to my father, Eddie, maybe [NON-ENGLISH] knows where the piggy bank. We don't find [NON-ENGLISH] piggy bank. And my father says, George, if you tell me the truth that you took it away and you bought ice cream and chocolate, I says, and will you buy me the Indian hat? Yes. I says, and you won't hit me? No. So what's a kid to say a lie? I said, yes. So my father was very embarrassed that I stole that piggy bank from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And two weeks, my father wouldn't even talk to me. I couldn't go out with him no more to buy ice cream. Every night, we went to buy a pint of ice cream. I think it was a nickle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two weeks later, [NON-ENGLISH] comes up and brings up the piggy bank, and says to my father, why did [NON-ENGLISH] say that he bought ice cream? I find it other the bed with a piece of cloth. I never got such a beating from my father for that, that I was a thief, and how he was embarrassed that his son is a thief and lie. That, I can never forget. I never forget that beating. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven, eight. Can you imagine? I can never-- I got a beating. I would never lie again to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? What was your mother like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very calm, calm, never-- everything was good. Everything, she was happy she had a child. When they were married in Hungary 16 years, there were no kids. And a year later, they come to Chicago, there I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did things change for you? When did-- you mentioned that your father died in 1921.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father 1921, he never was sick in his life. He's come home. He's got a headache. And we had a doctor. His name was Dr. [HUNGARIAN], a Hungarian. I remember. I got a very good memory. And forget it, then three, four days later, his headache was more and more. And they took him in the hospital, never got better. But a week later, I said to my mother, I never forget that day. I was 12 years old, 11, 12 years old. I says, Mommy, I dreamed that Daddy come home, and he had a white sheet on him. My mother start to cry. A half hour later, they call-- she start to cry. I went to school. I just-- I just had to walk across the street to North Hudson Street. And they called to the school that my father died. Then I knew that I was Jewish. I never know that I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know then?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that-- you know, then I saw that everything was as-- my mother said to me, you see, we're Jewish. We didn't want nobody to know we were Jewish there. But that's the reason we send you to that Catholic school or Germans, to learn German. And we had some business there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business? Oh, yeah, we had a bar there. And so, you know, when you said was I religious? No, we weren't. Why didn't they want anyone to know that they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You ask me questions I cannot answer you to honesty.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. So my father died in 1921, January 21st. My mother had a cousin there. And she had a son. And she didn't-- he was going to be some kind of person that my aunt invite. She was a cook. She cooked for one of the biggest Hungarian restaurants there, not far from the Lincoln Park. So when my father died, she come back with my mother together. But she went to Budapest. And she opened a printing factory-- for the print. But she had a brother. Her name was Fisher. And my mother come home to [HUNGARIAN], what I told you where we used to live first. And we lived there. Then later on, we send back in the 1920, over $26,000 in the-- in the American National Bank. And we got that money all back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we bought this land, 16 acres of land, and three acres of a wine garden. We had a wonderful life. And then I went to school. I had never-- I didn't like school. Tell Me about the transition for you. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Tell me about what it was like to go to Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come back from Chicago to Hungary, it was funny. I spoke Hungarian pretty good, with my parents. But I couldn't get to it. In America, we had everything different, you know? We didn't have that ice cream. We didn't have the bananas. We didn't have this, that. It was a little city. You couldn't even get-- right on the Yugoslav border, I show you where. And then later on, in the summer I was always on the farm. But I didn't like the work there. You know, you got to work-- the farmers got up 3:00 o'clock in the morning. And the wine garden was pretty good. And then I want to learn a trade. I learn to be a salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, it's different. You want to be a salesman, you've got to be three years a printer. I had to sweep the floor, wash everything down, make up the salesman room, clean his shoe, and everything. But I had a good mouth. And I was a good speaker. So a year later, my boss says, you don't got to-- we're going to get somebody else for you. I got no pay. I just got three meals a day there. And my boss said to me that, you're a pretty good salesman. He says-- I know the salesman name was Mr. Gross, good looking curly hair. He says, I think you're going to be the salesman now. And we're going to have somebody. You don't got to wash the floor. And that-- and after I was there three years to be a salesman, I said, I'd like to go over to Budapest with my aunt, who we come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: We liked each other very much. I used to visit her in Budapest. And my boss got me a job, just what I told-- just across the street from there for the temple. And the first floor, [INAUDIBLE], remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: You marked it in there, I was a salesman. I had a very good mouth. I could speak. And I don't think I was 20, 21 years old. I was the youngest salesman in Budapest who had a car. We are salesman traveling around the country, one part of the country. And then in the meantime, the numerus clausus was coming. I had to go over three months to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, hang on. Let's stop a minute there with work. What were you selling as a salesman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was selling women's buttons, and all those stuff what you need for dresses, you know? You know, [INAUDIBLE], and then you know what-- that they put it under the skirt here and everything. For a woman-- the button-- the [INAUDIBLE] button, with the [INAUDIBLE] buttons, something with that, wholesale.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] and [HUNGARIAN]. One was a German. [GERMAN] was a German. And [HUNGARIAN] was a Hungarian. He was a German, [GERMAN]. He was a very good boss.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I come back-- after the three months, I went to work. I want to get back my job. And [GERMAN] said-- they called me [HUNGARIAN], but they don't want to-- I couldn't have a Jewish name there. He says, I'm sorry. We can't have you back. It's numerus clausus, and the quota is not enough big that I can have so many Jewish workers here. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's-- had you intended to stay in Hungary? Were you committed to staying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I give up my citizenship. I never wanted to come back to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you give up your citizenship?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936, the worst time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why then? What made you do it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a wonderful life in Hungary. I had a wonderful life. With my mother went to-- we went to Italy, to Spain, warm places, and every places. I lived with a little king. I had a bachelor apartment they come to clean. Why should I leave something good there, when I got-- I got the best piece of stuff there. So I-- when I was in the work camp for three months, somebody had a messenger boy company. And I worked-- when I couldn't get back my job, first I went home from the farm. And then they said, Jew, you can have so much land, how much you can work yourself. I never learned how to work on the land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I come back to Budapest. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I worked for him two years. It was 1942, January, February, when all of a sudden come out that we have to go in. We've got to take us winter clothes and everything. They're going to take us out of the country. That was 1942, February or March, I don't know. Did you feel Jewish at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you identify yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. Then, I feel Jewish. When I was in trouble, then I really feel Jewish. You asked me a question, I tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin seeing changes in your life, and in the way you were treated in Budapest? Was that in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after 1940, when I come back from work, I couldn't get back my job what I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the quotas entail? Where they for all occupations, or only certain things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for the doctors, for the lawyers, yes, for the engineers yes, and for the salesman-- you know what they were pretty good salesman. And they could talk, they could sell a stomach-- a kid in a woman's stomach, a good salesman. But my boss always, when he teach me to be a salesman, he says, it's not the trick somebody comes to buy you a pair of shoes, sell them a hat. Then you're a good salesman. That time, I feel that I'm down in the dump, that I couldn't get back a job when I was the youngest salesman on the road. I was 19, 20 years old. And my mother died in 1921. Just bang.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, 1931, the same day she died with my father. My father January 21st. My mother died June 21st, the same day, exactly 10 years later. She was the 55 years old. She was never sick in her life. She was writing a letter in her hand. The pen fall out of her hand. She drop down dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in Budapest already by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then in Budapest. And I got a telegram that my mother was-- she died. I went right away home. And I didn't know what happened. I bought right away for home, a stone-- and stone that the whole thing was covered. And on her-- in her name, a stone this way, that's where she's-- but my father, you know, he's buried in Chicago. And now you saw that I got something that I want to bury in Israel. I want all three of us be one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're going to move your father and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we're not going to move them, no. You don't got to move them. It's all set.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. So you came back in 1940. And you got a job doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: A messenger boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a messenger boy for whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the messenger boy? This guy called up a letter to this lady. This lady called up a letter to the guy. And then they said that she wants to buy something. And this and that, there was some boy there, a good looking boy. He had the same job I do. His name was [HUNGARIAN], a good looking guy. And once a older lady called that he needs somebody to send, she was fall in love with this guy. And always, she always called number six. Then the boy, and he bought her all kinds of clothes. And that's a-- just want to tell you, it's how you had your luck. And after two years and two months, we had to go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how did you get notified that you were supposed to leave now in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a plaque out. There was everything out, at every corner, all the Jews this and this age, be ready to go to [NON-ENGLISH], showed the picture there. And get ready for the winter. For how long, we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much notice were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two, three weeks, we had notice, two, three weeks. A lot of them didn't go in. A lot of them didn't go in. They got false papers, you know, that they're not Jews. They have Jews. But then what I told you what happened with my cousins too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you-- what were you allowed to bring? Or what did you bring?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I had to bring with me, winter clothes-- boots, shoes, blankets, gloves, what you could bring. But how much can a man bring? Usually,we brought an extra boots, and this and that, you know? We didn't know where we're going to go. So the first place we was going in-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we got a train after [NON-ENGLISH], where we were liberated, and where my cousin kid got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's stay-- we're at the beginning still. So did you bring any personal items with you other than clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I left it by-- I had a cousin in Budapest, one of the brothers there, [HUNGARIAN]. He was decorated from the First World War. They couldn't touch him. He was a German army. But they couldn't touch him. So I left my mother's silver there, my rings, and this or that. They couldn't touch him. And they bombed the whole house. So nothing was left there. Everybody was left by [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you make any attempts to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Escape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Get out of doing this, or not go, or get--

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought it was going to be the same thing it was with the first three months, where nothing-- every three weeks, we have a day off. We come into Budapest. And some guys, there were lucky. They said, we'll bring ink. We'll bring paper. We'll bring pencil. They were a week, two, three days they were home in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what you were told. And what, in fact, did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I find, it wasn't true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. We had to go to [NON-ENGLISH]. There, they brought us there with the train. And then we went to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when I-- what I-- what went ask for water. And that Hungarian guy says, water? Hell for you, water. And he throws stones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NON-ENGLISH], who was the Sergeant said, you gotta give these guys water. They're working for the German Wehrmacht. So then we had water there. And we, from two months after that, they put us in-- in-- in the train, and took us to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the Sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 100 by 17. He was the Sergeant of all of them. He was not an officer. The officer was not with us. He was just [NON-ENGLISH] was. How long, we didn't get into Galicia. Then we got the officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who were you technically working for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you told? Was this you were working for the army, or you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: What they said that something comes, we got to unload it, over load it. Or we gotta fix up the railroad. We gotta work that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they classify you as prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered a prisoner? Or were you considered a conscript , part of the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of us, but later on, they give us a regular Hungarian Army uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uniform, but no bayonet, no nothing, just that and a belt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. So you went from [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Galicia too. That when I said I saw that a German soldier went in, take out a cow. And that woman had to be 19, 20 years old on his knee. [NON-ENGLISH], don't steal it. He takes and shoots that little woman. And then a little kids run out, about a year, two years old, and kills him too. And then when I said, the Germans said to him, [GERMAN], you do something with that. They were very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that in--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who did you see kill this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was he-- who-- I just him. Everybody saw he shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In there? Maybe two-- not even a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very quick stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very quick, and it was nothing to do. They packed us up and took us further.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was just a pit stop along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was actually transporting you? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Sergeant [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, were there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was guards, Hungarian guards with bayonets and everything. You couldn't run away, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Germans also? Or was it all Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no Germans there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact, really, with the local populations, other than--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: That incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had night time, we were-- the three days we were there, or two days, we were locked up in a barn. It was terrible. You got to beg to-- you had go out, you know? They put us about 50, 60, 80 people in a barn. And they left you there. They were going around. There was younger guys with guns and everything. So you couldn't escape from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of people who just refused to go when this call went out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I know a lot of them. They-- they had girlfriends. Or excuse me, a lot of Jewish boys had girls there, you know, what they call those-- no. Pimps, you know a lot of them, they have girlfriends there. They hide them. There was a lot of hide them. A lot of Jewish guys married Christians. You know, I told you. They didn't go. Later on, come in the law that you had a-- you had a white thing. We had a yellow, my two cousin's kids. A lot of them escaped, you know, who their friends where to go. They went to the village. Or you went someplace, just with my--
